risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Consented individuals were asked to complete baseline assessment visit (carbon monoxide (CO) breath testing and self-report measures) and then undergo randomization via random draws to either the Control Group ... or MTS ..." Comment: The study explicitly describes allocation by "random draws," which is a truly random method and is sufficient to indicate proper random sequence generation.

_evidence.method_quote: Consented individuals were asked to complete baseline assessment visit (carbon monoxide (CO) breath testing and self-report measures) and then undergo randomization via random draws to either the Control Group (Quit Line + 4 weeks Nicotine Replacement Therapy (NRT)) or MTS (MTS + 4 weeks NRT).
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were randomized by random draws to one of two groups.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Consented individuals were asked to complete baseline assessment visit (carbon monoxide (CO) breath testing and self-report measures) and then undergo randomization via random draws to either the Control Group ... or MTS ..." Comment: The paper describes randomization by random draws, but it does not state any concealment mechanism (e.g. central allocation, sealed opaque sequentially numbered envelopes, or identical containers) that would prevent recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment. Therefore allocation concealment is unclear.

_evidence.concealment_quote: Consented individuals were asked to complete baseline assessment visit (carbon monoxide (CO) breath testing and self-report measures) and then undergo randomization via random draws to either the Control Group (Quit Line + 4 weeks Nicotine Replacement Therapy (NRT)) or MTS (MTS + 4 weeks NRT).
_evidence.note: The article describes sequence generation/randomization via random draws, but gives no description of allocation concealment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Blinding was not used because many participants worked odd hours, had transportation issues or temporary housing, and it was necessary to allow them to decline participation prior to randomization if they could not make the MTS schedule." Comment: The article explicitly states that blinding was not used for participants/personnel. This trial also included several subjective, self-reported outcomes (e.g. DERS, ACS, FFMQ, meditation minutes, course evaluation), so knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence outcomes; therefore the dominant judgement is High risk.

_evidence.blinding_quote: Blinding was not used because many participants worked odd hours, had transportation issues or temporary housing, and it was necessary to allow them to decline participation prior to randomization if they could not make the MTS schedule.
outcome_categories: days smoked in first two weeks
objective_outcomes: days smoked in first two weeks

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Blinding was not used" ... "questionnaire measures were obtained" ... "The following self-report measures were obtained at various time points during the intervention" and "Smoking abstinence and questionnaire data were collected at three assessment visits". Comment: The study explicitly states that blinding was not used, and several outcomes were patient-reported/self-report measures (DERS, ACS, FFMQ, meditation calendar) assessed by the participants themselves, who were not blinded. Because the domain is judged at the level of the most vulnerable outcome, the presence of unblinded subjective/self-reported outcomes makes detection bias high, even though abstinence outcomes also included CO/TLFB confirmation.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: Blinding was not used because many participants worked odd hours, had transportation issues or temporary housing, and it was necessary to allow them to decline participation prior to randomization if they could not make the MTS schedule.

outcomes.label: number of days smoked in the first two weeks post-quit

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Pre-intervention attrition rates (dropout immediately after randomization) were 43% for MTS and 37.2% for Controls (p = .22). The most commonly reported reasons for pre-intervention attrition for both groups was lack of a phone, impermanent housing, or transportation issues. After these, the most commonly reported reason for pre-intervention attrition for MTS was scheduling conflicts, and for Controls was the desire to be in MTS." Comment: The paper reports very high post-randomization attrition immediately after randomization, with total loss well above 30% and some imbalance between arms (43% vs 37.2%). It also gives reasons for dropout that are plausibly related to treatment participation and acceptability. In addition, the article states that repeated self-report measures were analyzed on "report completers only," which is a post-randomization complete-case approach for those outcomes. These reported data are sufficient to judge attrition bias as high risk.

per_arm_flow.reasons: “Pre-intervention attrition rates (dropout immediately after randomization) were 43% for MTS” ; “The most commonly reported reasons for pre-intervention attrition for both groups was lack of a phone, impermanent housing, or transportation issues. After these, the most commonly reported reason for pre-intervention attrition for MTS was scheduling conflicts”

“Pre-intervention attrition rates (dropout immediately after randomization) were ... 37.2% for Controls” ; “The most commonly reported reasons for pre-intervention attrition for both groups was lack of a phone, impermanent housing, or transportation issues. After these, the most commonly reported reason for pre-intervention attrition for MTS was scheduling conflicts, and for Controls was the desire to be in MTS.”

_evidence.missing_data_method: intent-to-treat (NMAR) principle; failure to attend the 4- or 24-week assessment visits resulted in coding the participant as relapsed at that visit; report completers only for repeated self-report measures
_evidence.notes: Primary abstinence outcome was analyzed by ITT at 4 and 24 weeks; attrition data quoted here are pre-intervention dropout immediately after randomization, not per-arm 4/24-week missingness. Table 4 self-report analyses used only completers (MTS n=38, Control n=43).
